name: CBT Cognitive Conceptualization for Self-Sacrificing Grief
history: The patient experienced the death of a close aunt, whom they considered a mother figure. They were unable to fulfill her last request and were not present at her death, which likely contributes to ongoing feelings of guilt and regret. The patient has a longstanding pattern of prioritizing others' needs over their own, as shown by their recent choice to give up a personal plan to support a friend. This self-sacrificing tendency may serve as a way to cope with or avoid personal grief and difficult emotions.
intermediate_belief: If I don't always put others' needs before my own, I am failing as a friend and person. I must be there for others, even at my own expense, or I will feel guilty and inadequate. Taking care of myself is selfish and means I am not a good person.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't help everyone or make up for my past mistakes, it means I'm a failure and nothing I do will ever be enough. No matter how much I give, I can't escape feeling guilty and empty.
coping_strategies: The patient frequently sacrifices their own needs to support others, using this as a way to avoid facing their own grief and difficult emotions. They neglect self-care and personal fulfillment, which may temporarily reduce guilt but ultimately maintains feelings of inadequacy and emotional exhaustion.
situation: On the way to watch a movie, received a call from a friend seeking emotional support. Chose to give up the movie and talked with the friend for about an hour.
auto_thought: If I don't help my friend right now, I'll be letting them down. My own plans don't matter as much as being there for others.
behavior: I immediately cancel my movie plans and spend the next hour talking to my friend, focusing entirely on their needs and ignoring my own disappointment.
rationale: The report describes the patient as having a pattern of prioritizing others' needs over their own, especially in the recent situation of giving up a personal plan to support a friend. It notes self-critical thoughts and guilt related to perceived failures, as well as behavioral self-sacrifice and avoidance of personal emotions. The emotional impact section highlights guilt, sadness, and emotional burden, while the coping styles section discusses using service to others as a way to distract from grief. These themes directly inform the automatic thought, emotions, behaviors, and beliefs selected.
